The equilibria shown are: 1) phenol + HO⁻ ⇌ phenolate ion + H₂O, with boxes for pKa values below phenol and water. 2) acetate ion + protonated methylamine ⇌ acetic acid + methylamine, with boxes for pKa values below acetate ion and acetic acid.

B) For each set of reactants, draw in all lone pairs of electrons and show the electron movement by using curved arrows. C) Indicate whether the reactants or products will be favored at equilibrium by choosing the appropriate set of equilibrium arrows. question_text:
Rank the following sets of molecules in order of acidity (1 = most acidic).

First set: Molecules with protonated atoms (N, S, P, Cl, C) bonded to CH3 groups:
- Protonated N with two CH3 groups
- Protonated S with one CH3 group
- Protonated P with two CH3 groups
- Protonated Cl
- Protonated C with three CH3 groups

Second set: Protonated molecules bearing O, S, or N attached to a carbonyl or similar group, showing positive charge on oxygen:
- CH3C(=O)SH protonated at O
- CH3C(=S)CH3 protonated at S
- CH3C(=O)NH2 protonated at O
- CH3C(=O)CH3 protonated at O
- CH3C(=O)OH protonated at O
canonical_answer: First set: 4;2;3;1;5; Second set: 3;1;5;2;4

question_text: Met-enkephalin, an endorphin, serves as a natural pain reliever that changes or removes the perception of nerve signals. Label all of the functional groups present in Met-enkephalin. The structure shows multiple functional groups circled at different positions. Identify functional groups such as amine, amide, phenol, arene, carboxylic acid, and sulfide in the structure.
canonical_answer: amine; phenol; amide; arene; carboxylic acid; sulfide

question_text: Name the following molecules: 1) A branched alkane with substituents at positions 3, 4, 5, and 6 labeled with methyl and isopropyl groups. 2) A cycloheptane ring substituted with bromine at position 1 and a tert-butyl group at position 4. Provide the IUPAC names for both molecules.
canonical_answer: 4-isopropyl-3,5,6-trimethyloctane; trans-1-bromo-4-tert-butylcycloheptane

question_text: A) Draw the 4 major resonance contributors for the molecule shown in the first box. Partially completed structures are provided as a time-saver. Do not generate any additional charges. B) Draw in all lone pairs of electrons and use arrows to show the movement of electrons within the structure. C) Place a checkmark in the small boxes of the three structures that contribute the most to the resonance hybrid. D) Choose all the nucleophilic atoms in the structure at the bottom of the page.
canonical_answer: The three resonance contributors with checkmarks are the two structures in the top-left, top-right and bottom-right boxes; nucleophilic atoms circled in the bottom-right molecular structure are the oxygen atoms (two keto oxygens) and the nitrogen atom (N-CH3 group).

question_text:
A) Circle the structures that represent a conformation of 2,2-dimethylbutane sighting along any C-C bond. (Five Newman projections are given, including a 2,2-dimethylbutane molecular structure with carbons numbered 1 to 4, and Newman projections showing various substituents: Me, tBu, Et, H).

C) Use the above Newman projections to calculate the barrier to rotation of 2,2-methylbutane sighting along the C2-C3 bond.
canonical_answer: A) Second, third, and fourth Newman projections circled; B) Completed Newman projections with correct substituents (not explicitly described because answer is graphical); C) 3.6 kcal/mol

question_text: A) Draw in the substituents on the ring flipped conformers of each molecule (A and B) to predict which molecule is lower in energy. B) Show the Newman projection for each ring-flipped conformer, sighting along the C5-C6 and C3-C2 bonds. C) Select the chair conformation that is lower in energy for each molecule. D) Which structure is lower in energy (select one)? A or B E) Briefly explain your choice.
canonical_answer: D) A; E) A has fewer 1,3-diaxial interactions in its lowest energy conformation

question_text: (4 pts) One of the steps in fat biosynthesis is the hydration of crotonate to yield 3-hydroxybutyrate. The reaction occurs by addition of -OH to the si face at C3, followed by protonation at C2, also from the si face. A) Label the top face of each alkene carbon atom as re or si. B) Show the stereochemistry of the product and C) label C3 as R or S. The reaction scheme shows crotonate (H3C-CH=CH-CO2-, with C3 labeled as re and C2 labeled as si) converting to 3-hydroxybutyrate (H3C-CH(OH)-CH(H)-CO2-).
canonical_answer: A) Top face labels: C3 = re, C2 = si; B) Product stereochemistry: OH on C3 is up (wedge) with S configuration; C) C3 is labeled S

question_text: Describe two methods for preparing a methyl ketone from the corresponding carboxylic acid. Over the arrows below, provide the structure(s) of reagent(s) used for your proposed transformations. For your second method, write the mechanism of the reaction in which the C–C bond is formed. Bonus: Select the structure of a reagent for the oxidation of the alcohol to the carboxylic acid (accepted: CrO3(OH)2 species; also accepted K2Cr2O7, KMnO4).
canonical_answer: Method 1 reagents: 1. SOCl2 2. H3C-NH-OCH3 3. MeMgBr; Method 2 reagents: 1. MeLi (2 equiv) 2. H2O (work-up); Bonus reagent for oxidation: CrO3(OH)2 (or K2Cr2O7, KMnO4)

question_text: Propose a mechanism for the following transformation: furan + 2,2-dimethyl-1,3-propanediol in the presence of acid catalyst H+ (cat.) forms a six-membered ring ether product with HO group at the end of the propyl chain and a 2,2-dimethyl ring fused to oxygen. Bonus: Using a Dean-Stark apparatus would ensure a high yield for this reaction. TRUE / FALSE
canonical_answer: FALSE

question_text: Compound A has the molecular formula C6H10O3 and shows a strong IR absorption peak near 1710 cm⁻¹. When treated with I2 in aqueous sodium hydroxide, A gives a yellow precipitate. When A is treated with Tollen's reagent (Ag+), no reaction occurs. However, if A is treated first with water containing a drop of sulfuric acid a new compound is isolated and is found to have a 1H NMR spectrum which includes a downfield triplet at 9.5 ppm. Compound A shows the following 1H NMR spectrum: singlet (2.13 ppm, 3H), doublet (2.75 ppm, 2H), multiplet (3.95 ppm, 2H), multiplet (4.05 ppm, 2H), triplet (5.12 ppm, 1H). 13C signals for A: 205.1, 108.4, 64.4 (2C), 48.2, 30.1 ppm. Deduce the structure of A, and write annotated reactions to explain the outcome of each chemical test. Bonus: Calculate the degree of unsaturation for A using U = 0.5(2C - H + 2).
canonical_answer: Structure of A is a 5-membered acetal containing a methyl ketone side chain; test 1 (I2, NaOH): Iodoform test positive giving yellow precipitate; test 2 (Ag+): no reaction (no aldehyde for oxidation); test 3 (H+): acetal cleavage gives aldehyde with 1H NMR signal at 9.5 ppm; Degree of unsaturation = 2

question_text: Rank the following functional groups in order of decreasing reactivity in addition-elimination reactions by putting the letter corresponding to each structure in the boxes at right: I (acetate ester), T (acetamide), M (benzoyl methyl ester). The order should be from most reactive to least reactive.

Consider the reaction conditions and spectroscopic data and predict the structures of compounds A and B. The reaction: A starting cyclic ether-ester compound reacts with Ph3P-CH3+ Br- and NaH (1 equiv) to give Compound A and Compound B. 

Compound A IR: 3300, 3100, 2900, 1640 cm-1; MS: 100.09 (100%), 101.09 (6.5%) m/z; 13C NMR: 139, 115, 63, 34, 32, 26 ppm. 1H NMR: peaks at 6-0 ppm region including 1H(m), 2H(m) that exchanges with D2O, 2H(t, J = 7 Hz), 2H(q, J = 7 Hz), and 4H(m).

Compound B IR: 3060, 2980, 1720, 1683, 1620 cm-1; MS: 70.04 (100%), 71.05 (4.4%) m/z; 13C NMR: 198, 136, 128, 27 ppm. 1H NMR: peaks at 7-0 ppm including 2H(m), 1H(dd, J = 10.1, 2.0 Hz), 3H(s).

Predict the chemical structures of both compounds A and B based on the above data.
canonical_answer: Compound A: HO-CH2-CH2-CH2-CH=CH2; Compound B: CH2=CH-CO-CH3

question_text: Crellinose is a reducing disaccharide which undergoes mutarotation. Hydrolysis by either aqueous acid or alpha-arabinosidases give L-arabinose and D-glucose. Oxidation of crellinose with bromine water followed by acid hydrolysis gives L-arabinose and D-gluconic acid. Methylation of the bromine-water oxidation product, followed by acid hydrolysis gives 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-L-arabinose and 2,3,4,5-tetra-O-methyl-D-gluconic acid. Methylation and acid hydrolysis of crellinose gives 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-L-arabinose and 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-D-glucose. What is the structure of crellinose? Draw your representation using appropriate Haworth structures. Show your logic and explain the experimental data.
canonical_answer: The observation that crellinose is a reducing sugar and undergoes mutarotation implies that there is a hemiacetal carbon present. Since the glycosidic bond is cleaved by alpha-arabinosidase, the linkage must be of alpha configuration. Oxidation of crellinose produces D-gluconic acid, which indicates that the hemiacetal carbon is located on the glucose component of the disaccharide. Since acid hydrolysis of the methylated oxidation product yields 2,3,4,5-tetra-O-methyl-D-gluconic acid, the glycosidic linkage must occur between the anomeric carbon of the arabinose component and the sixth carbon of the glucose component (since there is no methylation observed on the C6 alcohol). The glucose portion of the methylation and acid hydrolysis product of crellinose is 2,3,4-tri-O-methyl-D-glucose because the alcohol on C5 is tied up in the ring bonded to C1. Therefore, the structure of crellinose consists of a pyranose L-arabinose linked by alpha-1,6-linkage to a pyranose D-glucose, as shown in the image.

question_text: The complete structure of JZ, a polypeptide with antibiotic properties, has been worked out as follows: I) Analysis of the hydrolysis products gave equimolar amounts of Leu, Orn, Phe, Pro, Val. (Ornithine, Orn, is a rare amino acid whose formula is shown.) II) Measurement of the molecular weight gave an approximate value of 1250. III) Sanger N-terminal residue analysis with 1-fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene gave only DNB-NHCH2CH2CH2CH(NH3+)CO2-. IV) Partial hydrolysis of JZ gave the following di- and tri-peptides: Leu-Phe, Orn-Leu, Phe-Pro, Val-Orn, Phe-Pro-Val, Pro-Val-Orn, Val-Orn-Leu. Explain the results of the Sanger experiment (statement III) and determine the structure of JZ. Write the structure using three-letter amino acid representations, and draw a box around it to indicate your proposed structure for evaluation.
canonical_answer: The results of the Sanger experiment indicate that no N terminus is present in the polypeptide since the FDNB did not react with any free alpha-amino groups other than the one on the Orn side chain. The structure of JZ is a cyclic peptide with the sequence: Orn-Leu-Phe-Pro-Val-Orn-Leu-Phe-Pro-Val-Orn, as enclosed in a cyclic structure.

question_text:
Outline a synthesis of the following compound starting from benzene, toluene, or aniline. You have at your disposal any reagents encountered in Ch 41a, b, or c, and may use any organic molecules containing four carbons or fewer. The target compound has a nitro-substituted phenyl group conjugated to a chalcone-like α,β-unsaturated ketone linked to a phenyl ring. Reaction scheme includes:
- Starting from toluene, nitration with HNO3 to form nitrotoluene,
- Oxidation with KMnO4 to nitrobenzoic acid,
- Conversion via 1) SOCl2 2) LiAlH(OtBu)3 to nitrobenzyl alcohol,
- From benzene to acetophenone with acetyl chloride,
- Treatment of acetophenone with 1) LDA 2) nitrobenzyl alcohol then heating to form the target chalcone structure.
canonical_answer: Pathway: toluene → nitration with HNO3 → nitrotoluene → oxidation with KMnO4 → nitrobenzoic acid → 1) SOCl2 2) LiAlH(OtBu)3 → nitrobenzyl alcohol; benzene + acetyl chloride → acetophenone; acetophenone + 1) LDA 2) nitrobenzyl alcohol then heat → target chalcone compound

question_text:
Propose a synthesis of the following molecule from succinic acid. You have at your disposal any reagents encountered in Ch 41a, b, or c, and may use any organic molecules containing four carbons or fewer. (Hint: A good synthetic route takes advantage of the symmetry present in the target molecule.) The target compound contains two allyl ester groups and a central diketone on a six-membered ring.
- Starting with succinic acid,
- Treatment with 2 equivalents of allyl alcohol in the presence of TsOH·H2O (or any acid) to form the di-allyl ester,
- Reaction with 1) NaH (2 equiv) 2) HCl/H2O workup (accept any base for step 1) to cyclize,
- Finally treatment with MeI (2 equiv) and K2CO3 (2 equiv) (or any other base) to alkylate, forming the target product.
- Also accept unmethylated diketone as product.
canonical_answer: Pathway: succinic acid + 2 equiv allyl alcohol + TsOH·H2O → diallyl succinate; diallyl succinate + 1) NaH (2 equiv) 2) HCl/H2O → cyclized diketone; diketone + MeI (2 equiv) + K2CO3 (2 equiv) → methylated diketone product. Also accept unmethylated diketone.

question_text: Rank the following carbocations in order of stability (1 = most stable). Four carbocations are shown with their structures and degree of substitution (2°, 3°) labeled. Numbers 1 to 4 are placeholders for ranking.
Rank the following alkenes according to energy (1 = lowest energy). Four alkene structures are shown. Numbers 1 to 4 are placeholders for ranking.

For each of the following reactions: A) label the nucleophile as lone pair (n), pi bond (π), or sigma bond (σ); label the electrophile as empty orbital (a), pi bond (π*), or sigma bond (σ*); use curved arrows to show attack of the nucleophile on the electrophile; show the product of each reaction.

A) HC≡C⁻ + (ethyl oxonium ion with positively charged oxygen) → ?
B) (A carbocation on a 6-carbon chain with a double bond) → ? (to form a cyclopentyl derivative with a positive charge)
canonical_answer:
A) nucleophile: lone pair (n) on HC≡C⁻; electrophile: pi antibond (π*) on oxonium ion oxygen; attack shown by curved arrow from nucleophile to electrophilic site; product: propargyl alkoxide
B) electrophile: empty orbital (a) at carbocation; nucleophile: pi bond (π) at double bond; attack shown by curved arrow from double bond to carbocation; product: cyclopentyl carbocation

question_text: Provide the major product of each reaction. Include stereochemistry. A) Reaction of bicyclic alkene with Br2, H2O producing a bromohydrin. B) Hydrogenation of methyl-substituted cyclopentene with H2, Pd/C. C) Hydroboration-oxidation of methyl-substituted cyclohexene using 1. BH3, THF; 2. H2O2, NaOH. Structures of reactants and reagents are shown in reaction schemes. Identify major stereochemical products.
canonical_answer: A) Bicyclic bromohydrin with Br and OH in trans arrangement relative to CH3 substituent as shown in drawing; B) Methyl-substituted cyclopentane with hydrogens in cis arrangement at former double bond as shown; C) Cyclohexanol derivative with OH and CH3 substituents in equatorial positions as major product shown in boxed structure

question_text: Provide an appropriate alkyne starting material, reagents and initial product for the reaction in the boxes provided. The number of carbon atoms in the starting material should equal the number of carbon atoms in the final product. Reaction scheme: Starting material is a cyclopentyl acetylene (cyclopentane with a terminal alkyne), reagents 1) Br2, H2O, THF; 2) H2O2, NaOH. The initial product box shows a cyclopentyl compound with a double bond and an OH group. The final product box is a cyclopentanone with an equilibrium arrow indicating no reagents necessary for this step.
canonical_answer: box_1=cyclopentylacetylene; box_2=Br2, H2O, THF; box_3=H2O2, NaOH; box_4=cyclopent-2-en-1-ol (or cyclopent-2-en-1-ol structure); box_5=cyclopentanone

question_text: Provide the missing reagents above each reaction arrow and the final product in the box. Reaction scheme: Ethyl (Et) linked to a methyl (Me) alkyne starting material. First arrow missing reagents above, handwritten options Na, NH3 or Li, NH3. Product is presumably the trans-alkene Et-CH=CH-Me. Second reaction arrow with reagents: 1) OsO4; 2) NaHSO3 / H2O (or alternative options H2O2 or KMnO4 (cold, dilute) or NaOH / H2O). Final product is a diol with stereochemistry indicated. Third arrow with reagent HIO4 leads to boxed product of two aldehydes: acetaldehyde (MeCHO) and propanal (EtCHO).
canonical_answer: arrow_1=Na, NH3 or Li, NH3; arrow_2=1) OsO4; 2) NaHSO3 / H2O (alternatively H2O2 or KMnO4 cold dilute or NaOH/H2O); box=ethanal + propanal

question_text: Provide an alkyne starting material and all necessary reagents to complete the following 2-step reaction. Starting box shows a methyl (Me) linked to ethyl (Et) alkyne. First arrow reagents: H2 with Lindlar catalyst. Second arrow reagents: CH2F2 and Zn(Cu). The intermediate product is a cis-alkene Me-CH=CH-Et. The final product is a cyclopropane ring with Me and Et substituents at two carbons.
canonical_answer: box_1=methyl ethyl alkyne; arrow_1=H2, Lindlar catalyst; arrow_2=CH2F2, Zn(Cu); box_2=cis-methyl ethyl alkene; box_3=cyclopropane with Me and Et substituents

question_text: Provide the mechanism and major product for each of the following reactions. Ignore stereochemistry. A) Reaction of isobutylene (2-methylpropene) with HBr (reaction arrow shown), including curved arrow mechanism showing protonation of the double bond and bromide attack. B) Reaction of cyclopentene with HBr in the presence of ROOR and heat (triangle symbol), including radical initiation (ROOR bond cleavage to 2 RO radicals), reaction of RO radical with HBr to form ROH and bromine radical, bromine radical addition to cyclopentene double bond, followed by HBr reaction giving the bromoalkane product.
canonical_answer: A) Protonation of alkene leading to tertiary carbocation, followed by nucleophilic attack by Br- yielding 2-bromo-2-methylpropane. B) Initiation: ROOR homolysis to 2 RO• radicals; RO• + HBr → ROH + Br•; Br• addition to alkene double bond forming cyclopentyl radical; cyclopentyl radical + HBr → cyclopentyl bromide + Br•, major product is bromocyclopentane.

A) Bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane with two bromines and one hydrogen reacting with NaCN in DMF. B) Methyl substituted cyclopentane with an unspecified leaving group reacting with azide ion (N3-) in DMF. C) Ethylene chloride (ClCH2CH2O) reacting with H2O. D) 1-chlorobutane reacting with H2O. E) Cyclohexylmethyl chloride reacting with potassium tert-butoxide (t-BuOK) in tert-butanol (t-BuOH).
canonical_answer: A) Bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane with Br and CN groups (3-exo-cyano-bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane) with bromine and cyano substituents shown (structure drawn). B) Methylcyclopentane with azide substituent (azidomethylcyclopentane). C) Ethylene glycol (HOCH2CH2OH). D) NR. E) Cyclohexene (unsaturated cyclohexane, Hoffman elimination product).

question_text: The compound shown below (structure of a bicyclic compound with nitrogen atoms) is not aromatic. Explain why not. Draw a picture or a model if this will help explain your reasoning.
canonical_answer: There are 2 nitrogen atoms that will be too close – molecule must bend to avoid steric clash. It is cyclic, has 4n+2 electrons, but is not planar.

question_text: A) Which compound is more susceptible to electrophilic aromatic substitution? (Shown: aniline (NH2 bonded to benzene) and anilinium ion (NH3+ bonded to benzene)) B) Which carbonyl compound is more susceptible to nucleophilic attack? (Shown: cyclohexanone and pentanal) C) What is the purpose of the FeBr3 catalyst in an electrophilic aromatic substitution halogenation? i. It serves as a radical initiator ii. It destabilizes the carbocation intermediate iii. It acts as a Lewis acid to activate Br2 D) Rank in order of reactivity for electrophilic aromatic substitution (1 = most reactive) for these compounds: benzene, chlorobenzene, N-methylbenzamide benzene, benzamide (notations as visible). E) Rank in order of acidity (1 = most acidic) for the following phenol derivatives: phenol, 4-nitrophenol, 3-nitrophenol, 4-aminophenol.
canonical_answer: A) aniline; B) pentanal; C) iii; D) 1=benzamide, 2=benzene, 3=chlorobenzene, 4=N-methylbenzamide; E) 1=4-nitrophenol, 2=3-nitrophenol, 3=phenol, 4=4-aminophenol

question_text: Draw the structure of the MAJOR product of the following reactions. If there is no reaction, write "NR". A) Reaction of 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene with HNO3/H2SO4. B) Reaction of benzene with isopropyl chloride and AlCl3. C) Reaction of 1,3-dichloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene with cyclohexylamine. D) Reaction of 1,4-dioxan-2-one (cyclic ester) with LiAlH4.
canonical_answer: A) 1,3,5-trimethyl-2-nitrobenzene major product by sterics; B) 1-isopropyl-3,5-dimethylbenzene (major by rearrangement), 1,3,5-trimethyl-2-isopropylbenzene also acceptable; C) No aromatic substitution (no aromatic substitution product) as major; D) 1,6-hexanediol (reduction of lactone to diol)

question_text:
The equilibrium constant for hydration of acetone is 2 x 10^-3, which translates into a small percent conversion to the following hydrate: acetone (CH3COCH3) equilibrates with its geminal diol hydrate (CH3C(OH)2CH3). The equilibrium constant Keq = 2 x 10^-3. Would you expect the equilibrium constant for hexafluoroacetone (CF3COCF3) to be smaller or larger than that for acetone?
Choose:
A) Keq greater than 2 x 10^-3
B) Keq less than 2 x 10^-3
canonical_answer: A

question_text: Draw a synthesis of the following product (a cyclohexene ring with a propyl substituent) from the given starting materials (cyclohexanone and 1-bromopropane). Additional reagents will be necessary. Each step in your synthesis must be clearly shown, but no mechanism is required.
canonical_answer: Convert 1-bromopropane to the corresponding triphenylphosphonium salt with PPh3; then treat with nBuLi to form the ylide; finally perform a Wittig reaction with cyclohexanone to yield the product alkene.

question_text: Furan is readily acetylated with acetic anhydride and a Lewis acid such as BF3. Predict the major regioisomer produced in this reaction and justify your reasoning with resonance structures. The reaction scheme shows furan reacting with acetic anhydride and BF3 to give an acetylated furan product. Provide resonance structures explaining substitution positions.
canonical_answer: The acetyl group substitutes at the 2-position of furan; resonance structures show stabilization of positive charge at the 2-position intermediate favored.

question_text: Circle the alkene that has the lowest energy among the four shown alkene isomers (structural isomers with different substituents on the double bond).
canonical_answer: The alkene is the one with a double bond between the second and third carbon counting from left to right, substituted with an isopropyl group on the third carbon (circled in the image).

question_text:
Provide products for each reaction (include all stereoisomers). Indicate if the products are achiral/optically inactive (A), racemic/optically inactive (R), meso/optically inactive (M), or chiral/optically active (C).

Reactions:
1) 1. BH3, THF 2. H2O2, NaOH on toluene derivative.
2) 1. CHCl3, KOH on bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane (norbornene).
3) 1. CH3CO3OH 2. H3O+, H2O on substituted bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane.
4) 1. Hg(OAc)2, H2O 2. NaBH4 on substituted cyclohexene with tert-butyl and ethyl substituents.
canonical_answer:
1) R: two stereoisomers including enantiomers (R,R and S,S) alcohols
2) M: meso dichlorocyclopropane derivative
3) C: chiral trans diol product
4) C: chiral alcohol product with Markovnikov anti addition

question_text:
Provide alkene or alkyne reactants and reagents for the following reactions. The number of carbon atoms in the reactant should equal the number of carbon atoms in the product.

Reaction 1: Conversion of an alkene (with a cyclopentane ring and a substituent) to a cyclopentane fused with a cyclopropane ring using reagents CH2I2 and Zn(Cu).

Reaction 2: Conversion of an alkyne-substituted bicyclic compound to an aldehyde-substituted bicyclic compound using 1. BR3, THF 2. NaOH, H2O2.

Reaction 3: Conversion of the same alkyne-substituted bicyclic compound to a ketone-substituted bicyclic compound using HgSO4, H2SO4, and H2O.

Reaction 4: Conversion of Et-C≡C-Me to CH3CH2-CH=CH3 using H2 and Lindlar's catalyst.
canonical_answer: Reaction 1 alkene: alkene with cyclopentane and substituent; Reagents: CH2I2, Zn(Cu); Reaction 2 alkyne: bicyclic compound with alkyne substituent; Reagents: 1. BR3, THF 2. NaOH, H2O2; Reaction 3 alkyne: bicyclic compound with alkyne substituent; Reagents: HgSO4, H2SO4, H2O; Reaction 4 alkyne: Et-C≡C-Me; Reagents: H2, Lindlar's catalyst

question_text:
Predict the principal products expected (if any) for the following reaction sequences. For each, select the structure you want to be evaluated.

A) Reaction of N-methylbenzamide with CH3CO2H at 25 °C for 10 hrs.
B) Conversion of cyclohexanecarboxylic acid by SOCl2 at 85 °C, then reaction with 1 equiv LiAl(OtBu)3 and H2O work-up.
C) Reaction of 2 equivalents of 2-hydroxyphenol and 1 equivalent of acetone with acid catalyst.
D) Reaction of isobutyric acid labeled with D at the carboxyl group with diazomethane (CH2N2) in ether.
E) Reaction of bicyclic compound (containing two ketone groups) with NaH and EtOH followed by H2O work-up.
canonical_answer: A) no reaction; B) cyclohexanecarbaldehyde; C) bis(2-hydroxyphenyl)propan-2-ol (chiral diol product); D) isobutyl diazomethane labeled ester; E) hydroxy-substituted ethyl cyclopentanecarboxylate

question_text:
A) The reaction coordinate diagrams for II and III are meant to represent 'extreme cases.' In the boxes on the right, draw the general functional groups that Loudon is referring to in II and III.

B) Loudon discusses two major factors that could affect the rate of carbonyl substitution. Use these two criteria to compare the rate of reaction of S-ethyl ethanethioate with NaOEt to the reaction of ethyl acetate with NaOEt, whose reaction coordinate diagram is shown in I of Figure 21.6. In the boxes below, explain how each factor would affect the reaction coordinate for the S-ethyl case, including annotated structures to support your arguments. Then sum up your conclusion by completing the statement below the boxes:

Circle One: The reaction of S-ethyl ethanethioate with NaOEt will proceed at a FASTER / SLOWER / SAME rate relative to the reaction of ethyl acetate with NaOEt.

C) Based on your arguments in part (b), inscribe an annotated reaction coordinate diagram for the thiol ester reaction in the box labeled IV above.
canonical_answer:
A) II = general functional group: R-C(=O)-NH2 (amide group) ; III = general functional group: R-C(=O)-Cl (acid chloride group)

B) Criterion 1: The resonance structures of S-ethyl ethanethioate show overlap of sulfur 3p orbital with carbon 2p, which is weaker than oxygen 2p overlap in ethyl acetate resonance. This results in S-ethyl ethanethioate being less stable than ethyl acetate, increasing the energy level of starting material.

Criterion 2: The SEt- group is a better leaving group than OEt- because sulfur is larger and better stabilizes negative charge in intermediate collapse. Also, pKa of HSEt is lower than HOEt, making SEt- a weaker base and thus a better leaving group.

Conclusion: The reaction of S-ethyl ethanethioate with NaOEt proceeds at a FASTER rate relative to ethyl acetate with NaOEt.

C) Reaction coordinate diagram IV should be annotated to reflect the thiol ester reaction coordinate with a lower activation energy peak compared to III and II, reflecting better leaving group ability and less stable starting material.

The pH - pKa = log([B]/[A]) calculation setup is shown with pH = 10.8 and pKa = 10.6. Percentages of [A] = 38.8% and [B] = 61.2% are also displayed as a reference.

Which of the compounds shown (three structures: a methyl acetate (CH3COOCH2CH3) with hydrogens labeled a, b, c; propanal (H3CCH2CH=O); and methyl propanoate (CH3CH2COOCH3)) fits the following spectral data? Mass Spec: m/z 88.05, IR: 1735 cm-1, 1H NMR: 4.13 ppm (quartet, 2H), 2.21 (singlet, 3H), 0.86 (triplet, 3H). Select the correct structure according to this spectral data.
canonical_answer: a

question_text: Richard Willstatter (1915 Chemistry Nobel Laureate) treated diamine A with excess methyl iodide, and then with excess Ag2O and heat, whereupon a hydrocarbon B, C8H8, distilled from the reaction mixture. Compound B rapidly reacted with excess Br2 under mild conditions. Treatment of compound C in the same way gave a hydrocarbon D, C6H6, which did not react with excess Br2. Identify compounds B, D, and E and in a sentence or two explain the different reactivity of B and D towards Br2 (Willstatter concluded from these experiments that compound D could not be an alkene). The question includes a reaction scheme showing compound A (a hexamethylenetetramine derivative with two dimethylamino groups), converted via 1. MeI (xs) and 2. Ag2O/heat into two hydrocarbons B and D. Hydrocarbon B is depicted as a non-aromatic 8-membered ring with alternating single and double bonds, hydrocarbon D is benzene. Hydrocarbon B reacts rapidly with excess Br2 producing E, a brominated product with eight bromines on the ring, whereas hydrocarbon D does not react with Br2. Bonus: What is the name of the reaction that converts A into B and C into D? The options or answers are not shown and should be given as free text.
canonical_answer: B: 1,5-dimethyl-1,5-diazocine (non-aromatic ring); D: Benzene; E: Octabromocyclooctane; Compound D (benzene) is very stable because it is aromatic and therefore does not react with Br2. Compound B is not aromatic and consequently is less stable and more prone to reacting with Br2. Bonus: Hofmann Elimination

question_text: Outline a sequence of reactions that would bring about the conversion of aniline into benzylamine. The question includes chemical structures showing aniline (aminobenzene) converting to benzylamine (benzylaminomethane). The reaction sequence shows: 1) NaNO2/HCl to give the diazonium salt, 2) CuCN to give benzonitrile, 3) LiAlH4 reduction, work-up with OH- and H2O, or alternately H2 and catalyst to get benzylamine. Provide the full sequence of steps.
canonical_answer: 1) NaNO2/HCl, 2) CuCN, 3) LiAlH4, 4) OH-/H2O work-up (alternatively H2 and catalyst for the reduction step)

question_text:
Arrange the amines within each set in order of increasing basicity in aqueous solution, least basic first. Indicate your answers by placing the compound numbers in the boxes for each series.

You are asked to use an extractive procedure to separate p-methoxyacetanilide (1) from p-methoxyaniline (2). Devise a dichloromethane-aqueous protocol by selecting the appropriate aqueous solution (A, B, C, or D) and then identify the location of compounds 1 and 2 in the biphasic system within the separatory funnel. Mark your answers as: enter 1 or 2 in the boxes for the organic (CH2Cl2) and aqueous layers, and enter the chosen aqueous letter (A, B, C, or D) in the box labeled aqueous solution.

Aqueous solutions to select from:
A) 5% NaOH pH 14
B) 5% HCl pH -0.8
C) 5% NaHCO3 pH 8.0
D) 25% NaCl pH 7.2

Bonus: Once you have separated 1 from 2, how would you retrieve the compound in the aqueous layer?
Hint: Treat the aqueous layer with base, then extract with organic solvent and collect the organic layer.
canonical_answer: organic=1; aqueous=2; aqueous_solution=B; bonus=Treat the aqueous layer with base then extract with organic solvent

question_text: Consider the structure of raffinose, a trisaccharide found in sugar beets, beans, cabbage, and other plants. Classify raffinose as a reducing or nonreducing sugar, and use abbreviated structures to support your claim. The structure shows that raffinose is a non-reducing sugar, evidenced by its lack of hemiacetal carbons (highlighted in the structure). Treatment with Br2 at pH 5-6 results in no reaction, indicating the acetal carbons are not oxidizable. Bonus: Name one of the constituent monosaccharides found within raffinose. Options: D-glucose, D-galactose, or D-fructose.
canonical_answer: Raffinose is a non-reducing sugar which is evidenced by its lack of hemiacetal carbons; Bonus: D-glucose, D-galactose, or D-fructose

question_text: A) Classify the following aldose as D or L. The aldose structures are shown with aldehyde group at the top and hydroxyl and hydrogen substituents along the carbon chain, with CH3 at the bottom. (2 pts) B) Which pair of the following aldoses are epimers and which pair are enantiomers? Aldoses A, B, and C are shown as Fischer projections with various arrangements of OH and H groups along the carbon chain. Compounds A and C are epimers. Compounds A and B are enantiomers. Bonus: Which pair of compounds are diastereomers? Choose B and C or A and C. (1 pt)
canonical_answer: A) L; B) Compounds A and C are epimers; Compounds A and B are enantiomers; Bonus: B and C

question_text: (+)-Trehalose, C12H22O11, is a non-reducing sugar found in young mushrooms and is the energy source for certain flying insects. It gives only D-glucose when hydrolyzed by aqueous acid or by α-glucosidase. It is inert to β-glucosidase. Methylation with excess methyl iodide gives an octa-O-methyl derivative that, upon hydrolysis, yields only 2,3,4,6-tetra-O-methyl-D-glucose. Explain the experimental data with annotated chemical structures. What is the structure of (+)-trehalose? Represent your answer using a Haworth representation. Bonus: Provide a common name for the polysaccharide shown on the cover of the quiz.
canonical_answer: The structure of (+)-trehalose is a disaccharide composed of two D-glucose units linked by an α-1,1-glycosidic bond, making it a non-reducing sugar that is inert to β-glucosidase and hydrolyzed to D-glucose by acid or α-glucosidase; it is represented by two D-glucose monosaccharides connected via the α-1,1 linkage in a Haworth structure. Bonus answer: starch

question_text: An aldopentose, O, is oxidized to a diacid, P, which is optically active. Compound O is also Ruff-degraded to an aldotetrose, Q, which undergoes oxidation to an optically inactive diacid, R. Assuming that O has the D-configuration, what are the structures of O, P, Q, and R? Represent your answers in each box by using Fischer projections displayed in the standard carbohydrate orientation. Bonus: List the reagent(s) necessary to effect Ruff degradation of compound O to compound Q.
canonical_answer: compound O = CHO-CHOH-HOH-HOH-CH2OH (Fischer projection with OH on right on second carbon, left on third, right on fourth); compound P = COOH-CHOH-HOH-HOH-COOH (oxidized diacid form); compound Q = CHO-HOH-HOH-CH2OH (aldotetrose with OH on right on second and third carbons); compound R = COOH-HOH-HOH-COOH (oxidized diacid of Q); Bonus = 1) Br2, H2O (optionally including Ca(OH)2 or CaCO3) and 2) H2O2, Fe2SO4 or H2O2, Fe(OAc)3
